import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== PART 6: INTERPRETABLE MODEL - EGFR EXPRESSION FOR EGFR INHIBITORS ==='"'"')
print()

# Focus on EGFR expression vs EGFR inhibitors (biologically motivated)
for drug in ['"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    x_egfr = expr_sub['"'"'EGFR'"'"'].reindex(y.index).dropna()
    common = x_egfr.index.intersection(y.index)
    xc = x_egfr[common]
    yc = y[common]
    
    r, p = stats.pearsonr(xc, yc)
    rs, ps = stats.spearmanr(xc, yc)
    
    # Simple linear regression
    slope, intercept, r_val, p_val, se = stats.linregress(xc, yc)
    
    print(f'"'"'{drug} vs EGFR expression:'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  n={len(common)}, Pearson r={r:.3f} (p={p:.2e}), Spearman r={rs:.3f}'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  Linear regression: slope={slope:.3f}, R2={r_val**2:.3f}'"'"')
    print()

print('"'"'=== PART 7: DRUG SENSITIVITY DISTRIBUTIONS ==='"'"')
print()
for drug in ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    # Bimodal test: check if distribution is bimodal (sensitive vs resistant)
    q25, q50, q75 = np.percentile(y, [25, 50, 75])
    iqr = q75 - q25
    # Fraction of very sensitive cells (IC50 < mean - 1 SD)
    threshold = y.mean() - y.std()
    frac_sensitive = (y < threshold).mean()
    print(f'"'"'{drug}: n={len(y)}, mean={y.mean():.2f}, std={y.std():.2f}, IQR={iqr:.2f}'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  Fraction sensitive (IC50 < mean-1SD): {frac_sensitive:.2%}'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  Q25={q25:.2f}, Q50={q50:.2f}, Q75={q75:.2f}'"'"')
    print()
"
' --timeout 60
[exit code: 0]

$ python3 -c "
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== PART 6: INTERPRETABLE MODEL - EGFR EXPRESSION FOR EGFR INHIBITORS ===')
print()

# Focus on EGFR expression vs EGFR inhibitors (biologically motivated)
for drug in ['ERLOTINIB', 'GEFITINIB']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    x_egfr = expr_sub['EGFR'].reindex(y.index).dropna()
    common = x_egfr.index.intersection(y.index)
    xc = x_egfr[common]
    yc = y[common]
    
    r, p = stats.pearsonr(xc, yc)
    rs, ps = stats.spearmanr(xc, yc)
    
    # Simple linear regression
    slope, intercept, r_val, p_val, se = stats.linregress(xc, yc)
    
    print(f'{drug} vs EGFR expression:')
    print(f'  n={len(common)}, Pearson r={r:.3f} (p={p:.2e}), Spearman r={rs:.3f}')
    print(f'  Linear regression: slope={slope:.3f}, R2={r_val**2:.3f}')
    print()

print('=== PART 7: DRUG SENSITIVITY DISTRIBUTIONS ===')
print()
for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'GEFITINIB', 'SELUMETINIB', 'TRAMETINIB']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    # Bimodal test: check if distribution is bimodal (sensitive vs resistant)
    q25, q50, q75 = np.percentile(y, [25, 50, 75])
    iqr = q75 - q25
    # Fraction of very sensitive cells (IC50 < mean - 1 SD)
    threshold = y.mean() - y.std()
    frac_sensitive = (y < threshold).mean()
    print(f'{drug}: n={len(y)}, mean={y.mean():.2f}, std={y.std():.2f}, IQR={iqr:.2f}')
    print(f'  Fraction sensitive (IC50 < mean-1SD): {frac_sensitive:.2%}')
    print(f'  Q25={q25:.2f}, Q50={q50:.2f}, Q75={q75:.2f}')
    print()
"

[exit code: 0]

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== PART 8: BINARY CLASSIFICATION (SENSITIVE vs RESISTANT) ==='"'"')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for drug in ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    # Binary: bottom 25% = sensitive, top 25% = resistant
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    
    # Use all samples with binary label (bottom vs top quartile)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)  # 1=resistant, 0=sensitive
    
    print(f'"'"'{drug}: n_sensitive={sum(y_bin==0)}, n_resistant={sum(y_bin==1)}'"'"')
    
    # Logistic regression
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    cv_auc = cross_val_score(lr, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  Logistic Regression AUC: {cv_auc.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc.std():.3f}'"'"')
    
    # Random Forest
    rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_r2 = cross_val_score(rf, X_scaled, y_cont.reindex(X_all.index), cv=kf, scoring='"'"'r2'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  RF Regression R2: {cv_r2.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_r2.std():.3f}'"'"')
    print()
"
' --timeout 120
[exit code: 1]

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]

expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== PART 8: BINARY CLASSIFICATION (SENSITIVE vs RESISTANT) ===')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'TRAMETINIB', 'SELUMETINIB']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='any')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    # Binary: bottom 25% = sensitive, top 25% = resistant
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    
    # Use all samples with binary label (bottom vs top quartile)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)  # 1=resistant, 0=sensitive
    
    print(f'{drug}: n_sensitive={sum(y_bin==0)}, n_resistant={sum(y_bin==1)}')
    
    # Logistic regression
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    cv_auc = cross_val_score(lr, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
    print(f'  Logistic Regression AUC: {cv_auc.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc.std():.3f}')
    
    # Random Forest
    rf = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_r2 = cross_val_score(rf, X_scaled, y_cont.reindex(X_all.index), cv=kf, scoring='r2')
    print(f'  RF Regression R2: {cv_r2.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_r2.std():.3f}')
    print()

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge
import warnings

# Load data

    print(f'"'"'  Random Forest AUC: {cv_auc_rf.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc_rf.std():.3f}'"'"')
    
    # Null model (random)
    null_auc = 0.5
    print(f'"'"'  Null model AUC: {null_auc:.3f}'"'"')
    print()

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestRegressor, RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))

print('=== PART 8: BINARY CLASSIFICATION (SENSITIVE vs RESISTANT) ===')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'TRAMETINIB', 'SELUMETINIB']:

    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    # Binary: bottom 25% = sensitive, top 25% = resistant
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)

    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    print(f'{drug}: n_sensitive={sum(y_bin==0)}, n_resistant={sum(y_bin==1)}')
    
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    # Logistic regression
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    cv_auc = cross_val_score(lr, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
    print(f'  Logistic Regression AUC: {cv_auc.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc.std():.3f}')
    
    # Random Forest Classifier

    cv_auc_rf = cross_val_score(rfc, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
    print(f'  Random Forest AUC: {cv_auc_rf.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc_rf.std():.3f}')
    
    # Null model (random)
    null_auc = 0.5
    print(f'  Null model AUC: {null_auc:.3f}')

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== PART 9: INTERPRETABILITY - LOGISTIC REGRESSION COEFFICIENTS ==='"'"')
print()

for drug in ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    lr.fit(X_scaled, y_bin)
    
    coef_df = pd.DataFrame({'"'"'gene'"'"': gene_cols, '"'"'coef'"'"': lr.coef_[0]})
    coef_df['"'"'abs_coef'"'"'] = coef_df['"'"'coef'"'"'].abs()
    coef_df = coef_df.sort_values('"'"'abs_coef'"'"', ascending=False)
    
    print(f'"'"'{drug} - Logistic Regression coefficients (positive=resistant, negative=sensitive):'"'"')
    for _, row in coef_df.head(8).iterrows():
        direction = '"'"'RESISTANT'"'"' if row['"'"'coef'"'"'] > 0 else '"'"'SENSITIVE'"'"'
        print(f'"'"'  {row[\"gene\"]}: {row[\"coef\"]:.4f} ({direction})'"'"')
    print()

print('"'"'=== PART 10: SINGLE-GENE vs MULTI-GENE MODEL COMPARISON ==='"'"')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for drug in ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    print(f'"'"'{drug}:'"'"')
    
    # Single gene models
    for gene in ['"'"'EGFR'"'"', '"'"'TPM1'"'"', '"'"'SALL2'"'"', '"'"'MICALL2'"'"']:
        if gene in gene_cols:
            gene_idx = gene_cols.index(gene)
            X_single = X_scaled[:, gene_idx:gene_idx+1]
            lr_single = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
            cv_auc = cross_val_score(lr_single, X_single, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
            print(f'"'"'  Single gene ({gene}): AUC={cv_auc.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc.std():.3f}'"'"')
    
    # Multi-gene model
    lr_multi = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_multi = cross_val_score(lr_multi, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
    print(f'"'"'  Multi-gene (all {len(gene_cols)} genes): AUC={cv_auc_multi.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc_multi.std():.3f}'"'"')
    print()
"
' --timeout 120
[exit code: 0]

$ python3 -c "
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== PART 9: INTERPRETABILITY - LOGISTIC REGRESSION COEFFICIENTS ===')
print()

for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'TRAMETINIB', 'SELUMETINIB']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='any')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    lr.fit(X_scaled, y_bin)
    
    coef_df = pd.DataFrame({'gene': gene_cols, 'coef': lr.coef_[0]})
    coef_df['abs_coef'] = coef_df['coef'].abs()
    coef_df = coef_df.sort_values('abs_coef', ascending=False)
    
    print(f'{drug} - Logistic Regression coefficients (positive=resistant, negative=sensitive):')
    for _, row in coef_df.head(8).iterrows():
        direction = 'RESISTANT' if row['coef'] > 0 else 'SENSITIVE'
        print(f'  {row[\"gene\"]}: {row[\"coef\"]:.4f} ({direction})')
    print()

print('=== PART 10: SINGLE-GENE vs MULTI-GENE MODEL COMPARISON ===')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'TRAMETINIB']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='any')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_all[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_bin)
    
    print(f'{drug}:')
    
    # Single gene models
    for gene in ['EGFR', 'TPM1', 'SALL2', 'MICALL2']:
        if gene in gene_cols:
            gene_idx = gene_cols.index(gene)
            X_single = X_scaled[:, gene_idx:gene_idx+1]
            lr_single = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
            cv_auc = cross_val_score(lr_single, X_single, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
            print(f'  Single gene ({gene}): AUC={cv_auc.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc.std():.3f}')
    
    # Multi-gene model
    lr_multi = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_multi = cross_val_score(lr_multi, X_scaled, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
    print(f'  Multi-gene (all {len(gene_cols)} genes): AUC={cv_auc_multi.mean():.3f} +/- {cv_auc_multi.std():.3f}')
    print()
"

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== PART 11: CROSS-DRUG GENERALIZATION ==='"'"')
print('"'"'Train on TRAMETINIB, test on SELUMETINIB (both MEK inhibitors)'"'"')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Get common cell lines with both drugs measured
y_tram = drug_sub['"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"'].dropna()
y_selu = drug_sub['"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"'].dropna()
common_cells = list(set(y_tram.index) & set(y_selu.index))
X_common = expr_sub[gene_cols].loc[common_cells].dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
common_cells2 = list(X_common.index)
y_tram_c = y_tram.loc[common_cells2]
y_selu_c = y_selu.loc[common_cells2]

print(f'"'"'Common cell lines: {len(common_cells2)}'"'"')

# Correlation between TRAMETINIB and SELUMETINIB IC50
r, p = stats.pearsonr(y_tram_c, y_selu_c)
print(f'"'"'Correlation between TRAMETINIB and SELUMETINIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}'"'"')

# Train on TRAMETINIB, test on SELUMETINIB
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_common)

# Binary labels
q25_t = y_tram_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_t = y_tram_c.quantile(0.75)
q25_s = y_selu_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_s = y_selu_c.quantile(0.75)

mask_t = (y_tram_c <= q25_t) | (y_tram_c >= q75_t)
mask_s = (y_selu_c <= q25_s) | (y_selu_c >= q75_s)
common_mask = mask_t & mask_s

X_cross = X_scaled[common_mask]
y_tram_bin = (y_tram_c[common_mask] >= q75_t).astype(int)
y_selu_bin = (y_selu_c[common_mask] >= q75_s).astype(int)

print(f'"'"'Cross-drug samples: {len(X_cross)}'"'"')

# Train on TRAMETINIB, predict SELUMETINIB
lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
lr.fit(X_cross, y_tram_bin)
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
pred_proba = lr.predict_proba(X_cross)[:, 1]
auc_cross = roc_auc_score(y_selu_bin, pred_proba)
print(f'"'"'Train TRAMETINIB -> Test SELUMETINIB AUC: {auc_cross:.3f}'"'"')

# Within-drug CV for comparison
cv_auc_tram = cross_val_score(lr, X_cross, y_tram_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
cv_auc_selu = cross_val_score(lr, X_cross, y_selu_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Within-drug CV AUC TRAMETINIB: {cv_auc_tram.mean():.3f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Within-drug CV AUC SELUMETINIB: {cv_auc_selu.mean():.3f}'"'"')

print()
print('"'"'=== PART 12: CROSS-DRUG GENERALIZATION (EGFR inhibitors) ==='"'"')
print('"'"'Train on ERLOTINIB, test on GEFITINIB'"'"')
print()

y_erl = drug_sub['"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"'].dropna()
y_gef = drug_sub['"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"'].dropna()
common_cells_eg = list(set(y_erl.index) & set(y_gef.index))
X_common_eg = expr_sub[gene_cols].loc[common_cells_eg].dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
common_cells_eg2 = list(X_common_eg.index)
y_erl_c = y_erl.loc[common_cells_eg2]
y_gef_c = y_gef.loc[common_cells_eg2]

r_eg, p_eg = stats.pearsonr(y_erl_c, y_gef_c)
print(f'"'"'Correlation between ERLOTINIB and GEFITINIB IC50: r={r_eg:.3f}, p={p_eg:.2e}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Common cell lines: {len(common_cells_eg2)}'"'"')

scaler2 = StandardScaler()
X_scaled_eg = scaler2.fit_transform(X_common_eg)

q25_e = y_erl_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_e = y_erl_c.quantile(0.75)
q25_g = y_gef_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_g = y_gef_c.quantile(0.75)

mask_e = (y_erl_c <= q25_e) | (y_erl_c >= q75_e)
mask_g = (y_gef_c <= q25_g) | (y_gef_c >= q75_g)
common_mask_eg = mask_e & mask_g

X_cross_eg = X_scaled_eg[common_mask_eg]
y_erl_bin = (y_erl_c[common_mask_eg] >= q75_e).astype(int)
y_gef_bin = (y_gef_c[common_mask_eg] >= q75_g).astype(int)

lr2 = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
lr2.fit(X_cross_eg, y_erl_bin)
pred_proba2 = lr2.predict_proba(X_cross_eg)[:, 1]
auc_cross2 = roc_auc_score(y_gef_bin, pred_proba2)
print(f'"'"'Train ERLOTINIB -> Test GEFITINIB AUC: {auc_cross2:.3f}'"'"')

cv_auc_erl = cross_val_score(lr2, X_cross_eg, y_erl_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
cv_auc_gef = cross_val_score(lr2, X_cross_eg, y_gef_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Within-drug CV AUC ERLOTINIB: {cv_auc_erl.mean():.3f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Within-drug CV AUC GEFITINIB: {cv_auc_gef.mean():.3f}'"'"')

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== PART 11: CROSS-DRUG GENERALIZATION ===')
print('Train on TRAMETINIB, test on SELUMETINIB (both MEK inhibitors)')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

# Get common cell lines with both drugs measured
y_tram = drug_sub['TRAMETINIB'].dropna()
y_selu = drug_sub['SELUMETINIB'].dropna()
common_cells = list(set(y_tram.index) & set(y_selu.index))
X_common = expr_sub[gene_cols].loc[common_cells].dropna(how='any')
common_cells2 = list(X_common.index)
y_tram_c = y_tram.loc[common_cells2]
y_selu_c = y_selu.loc[common_cells2]

print(f'Common cell lines: {len(common_cells2)}')

# Correlation between TRAMETINIB and SELUMETINIB IC50
r, p = stats.pearsonr(y_tram_c, y_selu_c)
print(f'Correlation between TRAMETINIB and SELUMETINIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}')

# Train on TRAMETINIB, test on SELUMETINIB
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_common)

# Binary labels
q25_t = y_tram_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_t = y_tram_c.quantile(0.75)
q25_s = y_selu_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_s = y_selu_c.quantile(0.75)

mask_t = (y_tram_c <= q25_t) | (y_tram_c >= q75_t)
mask_s = (y_selu_c <= q25_s) | (y_selu_c >= q75_s)
common_mask = mask_t & mask_s

X_cross = X_scaled[common_mask]
y_tram_bin = (y_tram_c[common_mask] >= q75_t).astype(int)
y_selu_bin = (y_selu_c[common_mask] >= q75_s).astype(int)

print(f'Cross-drug samples: {len(X_cross)}')

# Train on TRAMETINIB, predict SELUMETINIB
lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
lr.fit(X_cross, y_tram_bin)
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
pred_proba = lr.predict_proba(X_cross)[:, 1]
auc_cross = roc_auc_score(y_selu_bin, pred_proba)
print(f'Train TRAMETINIB -> Test SELUMETINIB AUC: {auc_cross:.3f}')

# Within-drug CV for comparison
cv_auc_tram = cross_val_score(lr, X_cross, y_tram_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
cv_auc_selu = cross_val_score(lr, X_cross, y_selu_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
print(f'Within-drug CV AUC TRAMETINIB: {cv_auc_tram.mean():.3f}')
print(f'Within-drug CV AUC SELUMETINIB: {cv_auc_selu.mean():.3f}')

print()
print('=== PART 12: CROSS-DRUG GENERALIZATION (EGFR inhibitors) ===')
print('Train on ERLOTINIB, test on GEFITINIB')
print()

y_erl = drug_sub['ERLOTINIB'].dropna()
y_gef = drug_sub['GEFITINIB'].dropna()
common_cells_eg = list(set(y_erl.index) & set(y_gef.index))
X_common_eg = expr_sub[gene_cols].loc[common_cells_eg].dropna(how='any')
common_cells_eg2 = list(X_common_eg.index)

y_gef_c = y_gef.loc[common_cells_eg2]

print(f'Correlation between ERLOTINIB and GEFITINIB IC50: r={r_eg:.3f}, p={p_eg:.2e}')
print(f'Common cell lines: {len(common_cells_eg2)}')

scaler2 = StandardScaler()
X_scaled_eg = scaler2.fit_transform(X_common_eg)

q25_e = y_erl_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_e = y_erl_c.quantile(0.75)
q25_g = y_gef_c.quantile(0.25)
q75_g = y_gef_c.quantile(0.75)

mask_e = (y_erl_c <= q25_e) | (y_erl_c >= q75_e)
mask_g = (y_gef_c <= q25_g) | (y_gef_c >= q75_g)
common_mask_eg = mask_e & mask_g

X_cross_eg = X_scaled_eg[common_mask_eg]
y_erl_bin = (y_erl_c[common_mask_eg] >= q75_e).astype(int)
y_gef_bin = (y_gef_c[common_mask_eg] >= q75_g).astype(int)

lr2 = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
lr2.fit(X_cross_eg, y_erl_bin)
pred_proba2 = lr2.predict_proba(X_cross_eg)[:, 1]
auc_cross2 = roc_auc_score(y_gef_bin, pred_proba2)
print(f'Train ERLOTINIB -> Test GEFITINIB AUC: {auc_cross2:.3f}')

cv_auc_erl = cross_val_score(lr2, X_cross_eg, y_erl_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
cv_auc_gef = cross_val_score(lr2, X_cross_eg, y_gef_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc')
print(f'Within-drug CV AUC ERLOTINIB: {cv_auc_erl.mean():.3f}')
print(f'Within-drug CV AUC GEFITINIB: {cv_auc_gef.mean():.3f}')

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load full IC50 matrix
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)

print('"'"'=== PART 13: DRUG SENSITIVITY LANDSCAPE ==='"'"')
print()

# Drug class analysis
drug_classes = {
    '"'"'EGFR inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"', '"'"'AFATINIB'"'"', '"'"'OSIMERTINIB'"'"', '"'"'LAPATINIB'"'"', '"'"'NERATINIB'"'"', '"'"'CANERTINIB'"'"', '"'"'PELITINIB'"'"', '"'"'SAPITINIB'"'"', '"'"'AZD8931'"'"'],
    '"'"'BRAF inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"'],
    '"'"'MEK inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'REFAMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'CI-1040'"'"', '"'"'ULIXERTINIB'"'"'],
    '"'"'PI3K inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'ALPELISIB'"'"', '"'"'BUPARLISIB'"'"', '"'"'PICTILISIB'"'"', '"'"'PILARALISIB'"'"', '"'"'TASELISIB'"'"', '"'"'IDELALISIB'"'"', '"'"'GSK2636771'"'"'],
    '"'"'AKT inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'CAPIVASERTIB'"'"', '"'"'IPATASERTIB'"'"', '"'"'MK-2206'"'"', '"'"'AFURESERTIB'"'"', '"'"'UPROSERTIB'"'"'],
    '"'"'mTOR inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'TEMSIROLIMUS'"'"', '"'"'OMIPALISIB'"'"', '"'"'VOXTALISIB'"'"', '"'"'DACTOLISIB'"'"', '"'"'OSI-027'"'"'],
    '"'"'CDK inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'PALBOCICLIB'"'"', '"'"'RIBOCICLIB'"'"', '"'"'DINACICLIB'"'"', '"'"'ALVOCIDIB'"'"', '"'"'AT-7519'"'"', '"'"'SELICICLIB'"'"'],
    '"'"'PARP inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'OLAPARIB'"'"', '"'"'NIRAPARIB'"'"', '"'"'RUCAPARIB'"'"', '"'"'TALAZOPARIB'"'"', '"'"'VELIPARIB'"'"'],
    '"'"'HDAC inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'VORINOSTAT'"'"', '"'"'PANOBINOSTAT'"'"', '"'"'ROMIDEPSIN'"'"', '"'"'ENTINOSTAT'"'"', '"'"'BELINOSTAT'"'"', '"'"'TACEDINALINE'"'"'],
    '"'"'BCL2 inhibitors'"'"': ['"'"'VENETOCLAX'"'"', '"'"'NAVITOCLAX'"'"', '"'"'OBATOCLAX MESYLATE'"'"'],
}

print('"'"'Drug class analysis - mean IC50 and coverage:'"'"')
for cls, drugs in drug_classes.items():
    available = [d for d in drugs if d in ic50_all.index]
    if available:
        vals = []
        for d in available:
            v = ic50_all.loc[d].dropna()
            vals.extend(v.tolist())
        vals = np.array(vals)
        print(f'"'"'{cls}: {len(available)}/{len(drugs)} drugs, mean IC50={vals.mean():.2f}, std={vals.std():.2f}'"'"')

print()
print('"'"'=== PART 14: DRUG-DRUG CORRELATIONS ==='"'"')
print()

# Compute pairwise correlations between targeted drugs
targeted_drugs = ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"']
targeted = ic50_all.loc[targeted_drugs]

print('"'"'Pairwise Pearson correlations between targeted drugs (IC50):'"'"')
for i, d1 in enumerate(targeted_drugs):
    for j, d2 in enumerate(targeted_drugs):
        if j > i:
            v1 = targeted.loc[d1]
            v2 = targeted.loc[d2]
            common = v1.dropna().index.intersection(v2.dropna().index)
            if len(common) > 50:
                r, p = stats.pearsonr(v1[common], v2[common])
                print(f'"'"'  {d1} vs {d2}: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}'"'"')

print()
print('"'"'=== PART 15: MOST VARIABLE DRUGS (BEST CANDIDATES FOR PREDICTION) ==='"'"')
stats_df = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/drug_ic50_stats.csv'"'"')
print('"'"'Top 10 most variable drugs (highest std in IC50):'"'"')
print(stats_df.head(10)[['"'"'drug'"'"', '"'"'n'"'"', '"'"'mean'"'"', '"'"'std'"'"']].to_string())
"

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load full IC50 matrix
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)

print('=== PART 13: DRUG SENSITIVITY LANDSCAPE ===')
print()

# Drug class analysis
drug_classes = {
    'EGFR inhibitors': ['ERLOTINIB', 'GEFITINIB', 'AFATINIB', 'OSIMERTINIB', 'LAPATINIB', 'NERATINIB', 'CANERTINIB', 'PELITINIB', 'SAPITINIB', 'AZD8931'],
    'BRAF inhibitors': ['DABRAFENIB'],
    'MEK inhibitors': ['TRAMETINIB', 'SELUMETINIB', 'REFAMETINIB', 'CI-1040', 'ULIXERTINIB'],
    'PI3K inhibitors': ['ALPELISIB', 'BUPARLISIB', 'PICTILISIB', 'PILARALISIB', 'TASELISIB', 'IDELALISIB', 'GSK2636771'],
    'AKT inhibitors': ['CAPIVASERTIB', 'IPATASERTIB', 'MK-2206', 'AFURESERTIB', 'UPROSERTIB'],
    'mTOR inhibitors': ['TEMSIROLIMUS', 'OMIPALISIB', 'VOXTALISIB', 'DACTOLISIB', 'OSI-027'],
    'CDK inhibitors': ['PALBOCICLIB', 'RIBOCICLIB', 'DINACICLIB', 'ALVOCIDIB', 'AT-7519', 'SELICICLIB'],
    'PARP inhibitors': ['OLAPARIB', 'NIRAPARIB', 'RUCAPARIB', 'TALAZOPARIB', 'VELIPARIB'],
    'HDAC inhibitors': ['VORINOSTAT', 'PANOBINOSTAT', 'ROMIDEPSIN', 'ENTINOSTAT', 'BELINOSTAT', 'TACEDINALINE'],
    'BCL2 inhibitors': ['VENETOCLAX', 'NAVITOCLAX', 'OBATOCLAX MESYLATE'],
}

print('Drug class analysis - mean IC50 and coverage:')
for cls, drugs in drug_classes.items():
    available = [d for d in drugs if d in ic50_all.index]
    if available:
        vals = []
        for d in available:
            v = ic50_all.loc[d].dropna()
            vals.extend(v.tolist())
        vals = np.array(vals)
        print(f'{cls}: {len(available)}/{len(drugs)} drugs, mean IC50={vals.mean():.2f}, std={vals.std():.2f}')

print()
print('=== PART 14: DRUG-DRUG CORRELATIONS ===')
print()

# Compute pairwise correlations between targeted drugs
targeted_drugs = ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'GEFITINIB', 'SELUMETINIB', 'TRAMETINIB']
targeted = ic50_all.loc[targeted_drugs]

print('Pairwise Pearson correlations between targeted drugs (IC50):')
for i, d1 in enumerate(targeted_drugs):
    for j, d2 in enumerate(targeted_drugs):
        if j > i:
            v1 = targeted.loc[d1]
            v2 = targeted.loc[d2]
            common = v1.dropna().index.intersection(v2.dropna().index)
            if len(common) > 50:
                r, p = stats.pearsonr(v1[common], v2[common])
                print(f'  {d1} vs {d2}: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}')

print()
print('=== PART 15: MOST VARIABLE DRUGS (BEST CANDIDATES FOR PREDICTION) ===')
stats_df = pd.read_csv('/workspace/drug_ic50_stats.csv')
print('Top 10 most variable drugs (highest std in IC50):')
print(stats_df.head(10)[['drug', 'n', 'mean', 'std']].to_string())

$ run_command 'python3 -c "
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY TABLE ==='"'"')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
summary = []

for drug in ['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"', '"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"', '"'"'GEFITINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='"'"'any'"'"')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    n = len(X_all)
    
    # Regression R2
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_all)
    
    ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
    cv_r2_ridge = cross_val_score(ridge, X_scaled, y_cont, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'r2'"'"').mean()
    
    rf_reg = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_r2_rf = cross_val_score(rf_reg, X_scaled, y_cont, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'r2'"'"').mean()
    
    # Classification AUC
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_scaled[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_lr = cross_val_score(lr, X_bin, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"').mean()
    
    rfc = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_rf = cross_val_score(rfc, X_bin, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='"'"'roc_auc'"'"').mean()
    
    # Best single gene
    best_gene = None
    best_r = 0
    for gene in gene_cols:
        r, p = stats.pearsonr(X_all[gene], y_cont)
        if abs(r) > abs(best_r):
            best_r = r
            best_gene = gene
    
    summary.append({
        '"'"'Drug'"'"': drug,
        '"'"'n_samples'"'"': n,
        '"'"'Ridge_R2'"'"': round(cv_r2_ridge, 3),
        '"'"'RF_R2'"'"': round(cv_r2_rf, 3),
        '"'"'LR_AUC'"'"': round(cv_auc_lr, 3),
        '"'"'RF_AUC'"'"': round(cv_auc_rf, 3),
        '"'"'Best_gene'"'"': best_gene,
        '"'"'Best_gene_r'"'"': round(best_r, 3)
    })

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary)
print(summary_df.to_string(index=False))
summary_df.to_csv('"'"'/workspace/summary_table.csv'"'"', index=False)
print()
print('"'"'Summary saved to /workspace/summary_table.csv'"'"')
"
' --timeout 300
[exit code: 0]

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, RandomForestRegressor
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, KFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression, Ridge, Lasso, ElasticNet
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== COMPREHENSIVE SUMMARY TABLE ===')
print()

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
summary = []

for drug in ['DABRAFENIB', 'ERLOTINIB', 'GEFITINIB', 'SELUMETINIB', 'TRAMETINIB']:
    y_cont = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    X_all = expr_sub[gene_cols].reindex(y_cont.index).dropna(how='any')
    y_cont = y_cont.reindex(X_all.index)
    n = len(X_all)
    
    # Regression R2
    scaler = StandardScaler()
    X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_all)
    
    ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
    cv_r2_ridge = cross_val_score(ridge, X_scaled, y_cont, cv=kf, scoring='r2').mean()
    
    rf_reg = RandomForestRegressor(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_r2_rf = cross_val_score(rf_reg, X_scaled, y_cont, cv=kf, scoring='r2').mean()
    
    # Classification AUC
    q25 = y_cont.quantile(0.25)
    q75 = y_cont.quantile(0.75)
    mask = (y_cont <= q25) | (y_cont >= q75)
    X_bin = X_scaled[mask]
    y_bin = (y_cont[mask] >= q75).astype(int)
    
    lr = LogisticRegression(C=1.0, max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_lr = cross_val_score(lr, X_bin, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc').mean()
    
    rfc = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
    cv_auc_rf = cross_val_score(rfc, X_bin, y_bin, cv=kf, scoring='roc_auc').mean()
    
    # Best single gene
    best_gene = None
    best_r = 0
    for gene in gene_cols:
        r, p = stats.pearsonr(X_all[gene], y_cont)
        if abs(r) > abs(best_r):
            best_r = r
            best_gene = gene
    
    summary.append({
        'Drug': drug,
        'n_samples': n,
        'Ridge_R2': round(cv_r2_ridge, 3),
        'RF_R2': round(cv_r2_rf, 3),
        'LR_AUC': round(cv_auc_lr, 3),
        'RF_AUC': round(cv_auc_rf, 3),
        'Best_gene': best_gene,
        'Best_gene_r': round(best_r, 3)
    })

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(summary)
print(summary_df.to_string(index=False))
summary_df.to_csv('/workspace/summary_table.csv', index=False)
print()
print('Summary saved to /workspace/summary_table.csv')

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('"'"'ignore'"'"')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('"'"'/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv'"'"', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '"'"'/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
    '"'"'/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv'"'"',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('"'"'=== PART 16: BIOLOGICAL INTERPRETATION ==='"'"')
print()

# EGFR expression vs DABRAFENIB (BRAF inhibitor)
# High EGFR expression is a known resistance mechanism to BRAF inhibitors
y_dab = drug_sub['"'"'DABRAFENIB'"'"'].dropna()
x_egfr = expr_sub['"'"'EGFR'"'"'].reindex(y_dab.index).dropna()
common = x_egfr.index.intersection(y_dab.index)
r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_egfr[common], y_dab[common])
print(f'"'"'EGFR expression vs DABRAFENIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Interpretation: Higher EGFR expression -> higher IC50 (more resistant)'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  This is consistent with EGFR-mediated resistance to BRAF inhibitors'"'"')
print()

# SALL2 vs MEK inhibitors
for drug in ['"'"'TRAMETINIB'"'"', '"'"'SELUMETINIB'"'"']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    x_sall2 = expr_sub['"'"'SALL2'"'"'].reindex(y.index).dropna()
    common = x_sall2.index.intersection(y.index)
    r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_sall2[common], y[common])
    print(f'"'"'SALL2 expression vs {drug} IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}'"'"')
print()

# TPM1 vs ERLOTINIB
y_erl = drug_sub['"'"'ERLOTINIB'"'"'].dropna()
x_tpm1 = expr_sub['"'"'TPM1'"'"'].reindex(y_erl.index).dropna()
common = x_tpm1.index.intersection(y_erl.index)
r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_tpm1[common], y_erl[common])
print(f'"'"'TPM1 expression vs ERLOTINIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}'"'"')
print()

print('"'"'=== PART 17: FULL IC50 MATRIX STATISTICS ==='"'"')
print()
print(f'"'"'Total drugs: {ic50_all.shape[0]}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Total cell lines: {ic50_all.shape[1]}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Total measurements: {ic50_all.notna().sum().sum()}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'Missing fraction: {ic50_all.isna().sum().sum() / ic50_all.size:.1%}'"'"')
print()

# Distribution of IC50 values
all_vals = ic50_all.values.flatten()
all_vals = all_vals[~np.isnan(all_vals)]
print(f'"'"'Overall IC50 distribution (log scale):'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Mean: {all_vals.mean():.2f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Std: {all_vals.std():.2f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Min: {all_vals.min():.2f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Max: {all_vals.max():.2f}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Fraction < 0 (very sensitive): {(all_vals < 0).mean():.1%}'"'"')
print(f'"'"'  Fraction > 5 (resistant): {(all_vals > 5).mean():.1%}'"'"')
"
' --timeout 60
[exit code: 0]

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Load data
targeted = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/b6fbd3ad6519/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
ic50_all = pd.read_csv('/workspace/data/3704868eefc2/dataset_003/file_001.csv', index_col=0)
expr_files = [
    '/workspace/data/5edb69105e85/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/66965ce89fb7/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/0942403f8e84/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
    '/workspace/data/757f1ee62dba/dataset_002/file_001.csv',
]
dfs = [pd.read_csv(f, index_col=0) for f in expr_files]
expr = pd.concat(dfs, axis=1)
expr = expr.loc[:, ~expr.columns.duplicated()]

targeted_T = targeted.T
overlap_cells = list(set(expr.index) & set(targeted_T.index))
expr_sub = expr.loc[overlap_cells]
drug_sub = targeted_T.loc[overlap_cells]
gene_cols = list(expr.columns)

print('=== PART 16: BIOLOGICAL INTERPRETATION ===')
print()

# EGFR expression vs DABRAFENIB (BRAF inhibitor)
# High EGFR expression is a known resistance mechanism to BRAF inhibitors
y_dab = drug_sub['DABRAFENIB'].dropna()
x_egfr = expr_sub['EGFR'].reindex(y_dab.index).dropna()
common = x_egfr.index.intersection(y_dab.index)
r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_egfr[common], y_dab[common])
print(f'EGFR expression vs DABRAFENIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}')
print(f'  Interpretation: Higher EGFR expression -> higher IC50 (more resistant)')
print(f'  This is consistent with EGFR-mediated resistance to BRAF inhibitors')
print()

# SALL2 vs MEK inhibitors
for drug in ['TRAMETINIB', 'SELUMETINIB']:
    y = drug_sub[drug].dropna()
    x_sall2 = expr_sub['SALL2'].reindex(y.index).dropna()
    common = x_sall2.index.intersection(y.index)
    r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_sall2[common], y[common])
    print(f'SALL2 expression vs {drug} IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}')
print()

# TPM1 vs ERLOTINIB
y_erl = drug_sub['ERLOTINIB'].dropna()
x_tpm1 = expr_sub['TPM1'].reindex(y_erl.index).dropna()
common = x_tpm1.index.intersection(y_erl.index)
r, p = stats.pearsonr(x_tpm1[common], y_erl[common])
print(f'TPM1 expression vs ERLOTINIB IC50: r={r:.3f}, p={p:.2e}, n={len(common)}')
print()

print('=== PART 17: FULL IC50 MATRIX STATISTICS ===')
print()
print(f'Total drugs: {ic50_all.shape[0]}')
print(f'Total cell lines: {ic50_all.shape[1]}')
print(f'Total measurements: {ic50_all.notna().sum().sum()}')
print(f'Missing fraction: {ic50_all.isna().sum().sum() / ic50_all.size:.1%}')
print()

# Distribution of IC50 values
all_vals = ic50_all.values.flatten()
all_vals = all_vals[~np.isnan(all_vals)]
print(f'Overall IC50 distribution (log scale):')
print(f'  Mean: {all_vals.mean():.2f}')
print(f'  Std: {all_vals.std():.2f}')
print(f'  Min: {all_vals.min():.2f}')
print(f'  Max: {all_vals.max():.2f}')
print(f'  Fraction < 0 (very sensitive): {(all_vals < 0).mean():.1%}')
print(f'  Fraction > 5 (resistant): {(all_vals > 5).mean():.1%}')
"

[exit code: 0]

1|# Fetched page: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8550985/
2|# Content-Type: text/xml; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|A novel device for treatment of osteonecrosis of femoral head: Feasibility and preliminary efficacy of animal study
5|
6|Abstract
7|
8|Interruption of blood supply will lead to necrosis of body tissues, such as osteonecrosis of femoral head (ONFH). Vascularization has always been regarded as one of the biggest challenges in tissue engineering. In the current study, a novel device was proposed to reconstruct blood supply of necrotic femoral head.
9|Cryo-insult with liquid nitrogen method was adopted to establish the ONFH model. In experimental group, a novel scaffold carrying vascular bundle was implanted into the necrotic femoral head after decompression and the transplanted vascular bundles were anastomosed with the existing blood vessels around the hip. In control group, a traditional porous scaffold was inserted alone without vessels. Feasibility of this strategy was verified by animal experiments. Micro-CT analysis and histological evaluation were performed to investigate its preliminary efficacy.
10|Feasibility of this innovative treatment strategy had been successfully verified in animal experiments. In the area of necrosis repair, more bone tissue grew into the scaffold in experimental group than the control group evaluated by Micro-CT (three months: 29.66% VS 20.35%, P＜0.05; six months: 30.47% VS 25.10%, P＜0.05) and histological analysis (24.71% VS 16.45%, P＜0.05 ​at three months; 31.01% VS 20.60%, P＜0.05 ​at six months). Implanted vascular bundles had the potential to branch out many branches in the osteonecrosis repair area to facilitate blood supply reconstruction and bone repair.
11|This study proposed a novel device with clinical application prospects in the treatment of ONFH. It has the potential to provide new possibilities for rebuilding the blood supply of femoral head and repairing osteonecrosis.
12|The novel device proposed in this study has the potential to be applied to the treatment of early femoral head necrosis.
13|
14|Introduction
15|
16|As a highly vascularized tissue, the development, survival and regeneration of bone depend on its vasculature [ [1] , [2] , [3] , [4] ]. Destruction of vasculature in femoral head will lead to osteonecrosis of femoral head (ONFH). It can occur due to a variety of reasons. Corticosteroids use, for instance, is one of the most common causes [ [5] , [6] , [7] ]. Currently in global treatment of COVID-19 pandemic, the application of corticosteroids has been proven to be effective in reducing mortality rate of critically ill patients [ 8 ]. World Health Organization (WHO) also recommends systemic corticosteroids use for severe COVID-19 patients [ 9 ]. However, this may also make these patients face a higher risk of sequelae of ONFH, referring to the incidence of steroid-induced femoral head necrosis (24%) in the treatment of severe acute respiratory syndrome (SARS) which broke out in 2003 [ [10] , [11] , [12] ]. It is foreseeable that with global spread of COVID-19 pandemic and application of corticosteroids, the number of patients diagnosed with ONFH will also significantly increase in the near future. Although there are many hip preservation interventions to prevent disease progression in treatment of ONFH, effective reconstruction of blood supply in the femoral head is still a major challenge in clinical and scientific research. In the current study we proposed a novel method to reconstruct blood supply for the necrotic femoral head by implanting a newly designed scaffold as carrier of transplanted vascular bundles and vascular anastomosis. The feasibility and preliminary efficacy for treatment of ONFH was further investigated in animal study.
17|
18|Materials and methods
19|
20|Design and fabrication of novel/traditional scaffold
21|
22|A novel device for treatment of ONFH was designed: porous titanium scaffold (pore size ​= ​400 ​μm) with an internal U-shaped channel was designed as carrier of transplanted blood vessels in the experimental group (Porous titanium scaffold with vessels group, PTSV group, Fig. 1 A). An open window was located on the side of top of scaffold ( Fig. 1 A) to ensure the successful insertion of vessels. The end of scaffold was designed as a solid structure with threads to achieve anchoring in cortical bone after implantation. Traditional porous titanium scaffold without U-shaped channel, open window or vessels was applied in the control group (porous titanium scaffold group, PTS group, Fig. 1 B) to simulate the effect of tantalum rods used in clinical practice. Scaffolds were fabricated with Ti6Al4V power by selective laser melting method (SLM; Concept Laser, Lichtenfels, Germany). Parameters of scaffold were set according to the measurement results of proximal femur of sheep in preliminary experiment: diameter of porous part (8 ​mm) and thread part (10 ​mm), total length (41–50 ​mm) in both group; diameter of the U-shaped channel (2.5 ​mm). In PTSV group, the saphenous artery of the animal was taken as a graft vessel. The vascular bundle was implanted into the experimental scaffold under the protection of catheter and guide wire. Then, scaffold carrying vascular bundle was implanted into the necrotic femoral head after decompression and the two ends of the transplanted vascular bundle were respectively anastomosed with the branches of the lateral femoral circumflex artery and vein to form an arteriovenous loop ( Fig. 1 A). In PTS group, scaffold was inserted alone without vessels ( Fig. 1 B). Fig. 1 A: a novel method to reconstruct blood supply for the necrotic femoral head by implanting a porous scaffold as carrier of transplanted vascular bundles and vascular anastomosis in PTSV group. B: Traditional porous scaffold inserted alone without vessels in PTS group. Fig. 1
23|
24|A: a novel method to reconstruct blood supply for the necrotic femoral head by implanting a porous scaffold as carrier of transplanted vascular bundles and vascular anastomosis in PTSV group. B: Traditional porous scaffold inserted alone without vessels in PTS group.
25|
26|Animal experiments
27|
28|All procedures strictly followed international animal protection guidelines and were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Fuwai Hospital (NO: 0080-2-30-HX(X)). Fifteen adult female Small Tailed Han sheep (age 24–36months, weight 60–85 ​kg) were used in animal experiment. Cryo-insult with liquid nitrogen method was adopted to establish the ONFH model in right hind limb of sheep as previously described [ 13 , 14 ]. Briefly, after successful anesthesia, the surgical limbs were prepared and disinfected. Posterior side of femoral head–neck junction was exposed. A tunnel (3 ​mm in diameter and 12 ​mm in depth) was induced by drilling from head–neck junction towards the weight-bearing area of femoral head ( Fig. 2 A). A cryoprobe was then inserted into the tunnel to induce cryogenic lesion ( Fig. 2 B). The cryo-insult was conducted by three freeze–rewarm cycles. After sealing the tunnel with bone wax, the incision was closed with 3–0 sutures. Postoperatively, experimental animals could move freely without restriction. Fig. 2 Establishment of ONFH model by using cryo-insult method. intraoperative drilling (A); cryo-insult using liquid nitrogen gun and cryoprobe (B); Von-Gieson staining: bone lacunae in trabecula of normal femoral head filled with deeply stained osteocyte nuclei (C); trabecula of cryo-insult side filled with many empty osteocyte lacunae (D). thick black arrow: femoral head; thin white arrow: empty osteocyte lacunae. Fig. 2
29|
30|Establishment of ONFH model by using cryo-insult method. intraoperative drilling (A); cryo-insult using liquid nitrogen gun and cryoprobe (B); Von-Gieson staining: bone lacunae in trabecula of normal femoral head filled with deeply stained osteocyte nuclei (C); trabecula of cryo-insult side filled with many empty osteocyte lacunae (D). thick black arrow: femoral head; thin white arrow: empty osteocyte lacunae.
31|
32|Three animals were sacrificed, and bilateral femoral head were harvested to verify the reliability of this model through histological examination at one month postoperatively. The remaining twelve animals were randomly divided into two groups (n ​= ​6 for each group) and received scaffold implantation procedure. In the PTSV group, after successful anesthesia, the surgical limbs were prepared and disinfected. A longitudinal incision was made on inner side of left hind limb. Saphenous artery over 20 ​cm was obtained as transplanted blood vessel following careful separation and ligation of the branches ( Fig. 3 A/B). Under protection of catheter, the transplanted saphenous artery was transferred into the novel scaffold with U-shaped channel for future use ( Fig. 3 C). An arc incision was made in front of greater trochanter of the right hind limb and the cortex at base of greater trochanter was exposed by separation. Under fluoroscopy, a guide needle was implanted into the necrotic area through lateral cortex, and a hollow drill with the same diameter as scaffold was used to remove the necrotic bone in femoral head to prepare the core decompression channel for scaffold implantation. Vastus lateralis was detached from its origin of greater trochanter and retracted distally to expose transverse branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery and its accompanying veins. The experimental scaffold carrying transplanted vessel was then inserted into core decompression channel, ensuring that the top window faced downward ( Fig. 1 A). Finally, the two ends of graft vessel were anastomosed with transverse branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery and its accompanying vein ( Fig. 3 D). The pulsation and patency of anastomosed blood vessels were checked again before closing the wound in anatomical layers. Cefazolin (50 ​mg/kg) was administered intramuscularly during surgery preparation and 3 days after surgery (twice a day) for prophylactic use. Papaverine and low molecular weight heparin were used for three days after operation to prevent arterial crisis and thrombosis. In PTS group (control), after elimination of necrotic bone and preparation of core decompression channel, traditional porous scaffold was directly inserted without vessels. Animals were sacrificed for imaging and histological evaluation at three and six months after surgery. Fig. 3 A/B: exposure and separation of saphenous artery; C: scaffold carrying vascular bundle; D: graft vessel anastomosed with transverse branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery and its accompanying vein. black arrow: transplanted saphenous artery. Fig. 3
33|
34|A/B: exposure and separation of saphenous artery; C: scaffold carrying vascular bundle; D: graft vessel anastomosed with transverse branch of lateral femoral circumflex artery and its accompanying vein. black arrow: transplanted saphenous artery.
35|
36|Micro-CT analysis
37|
38|Samples of proximal femur were retrieved and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde solution. Specimens were scanned by Siemens Inveon Micro-CT/PET (Siemens, Berlin, Germany. Resolution of 10 ​mm, Voltage of 60 ​kV, Current of 400 ​mA). Images were reconstructed and analyzed with Inveon analysis workstation. To evaluate bone ingrowth of implanted scaffolds, the scaffold area (top side, 15 ​mm in height) was selected as region of interest (ROI) for Micro-CT analysis. Bone tissue was separated from high-density titanium scaffold by using the threshold function.
39|
40|Histological evaluation
41|
42|After Micro-CT analysis, proximal femur samples were further cut along head–neck junction to obtain femoral head for subsequent histological evaluation of bone and vascular ingrowth. Femoral head were embedded in methyl methacrylate after dehydrated in a graded ethanol solution. Specimens were cut along coronal plane of femoral head by using Leica Microtome (Wetzlar, Germany). Sections were further stained with Von-Gieson staining or toluidine blue staining. Since it was the top of scaffold which replaced original osteonecrotic area, we selected this part (top side, 5 ​mm in height) as the region of interest (ROI) i

3|
4|A Comprehensive Scientific Survey of Excipients Used in Currently Marketed, Therapeutic Biological Drug Products
5|
6|Abstract
7|
8|The steady development of biotechnology-derived therapeutic biologics over the last few decades has generated drugs that are now standard medical treatments for a range of indications. While the development of protein products has surged in recent years, the formulation and delivery of these complex molecules have relied on drug-specific studies and, in some instances, data from non-proteinaceous drug products. The commonalities, trends, and gaps in excipient technologies used to support the development of therapeutic proteins largely remain unexplored due to the drug-specific nature of many formulations.
9|Using a comprehensive and relational database approach, we aimed to provide a scientific survey of all approved or licensed biotechnology-derived drug products with the goal of providing evidence-based information on common attributes and trending features in protein product excipients. We examined 665 formulations, and 395 unique formulations based on having unique excipients within them, that supported 211 therapeutic proteins as of June 2020.
10|We report the prevalence of each excipient class and excipient chemical used in eight different drug types including monoclonal antibodies, antibody conjugates, cytokines and growth factors, enzymes, polypeptide hormones, pulmonary surfactants, recombinant fusion proteins, and toxins. We also report the prevalence by excipient type among all therapeutic proteins, in the context of each drug’s recommended pH range, concentration ranges for excipients, and route of administration.
11|The results of our analyses indicate certain excipients common to monoclonal antibodies, cytokines, and polypeptide hormones. We also report on excipients unique to protein drug products, such as amino acids, solubilizers, and lyoprotectants. Overall, our report summarizes the current landscape of excipients used in marketed biotechnology-derived therapeutic biologic products.
12|
13|Introduction
14|
15|Biotechnology-derived drug products are produced from biological sources including human, animal, plant, or microbial and can be composed of proteins, nucleic acids, or a combination ( 1 ). Protein molecules are inherently unstable outside of their natural biochemical environment. For this reason, proteins developed as therapeutic agents are carefully characterized and stabilized to deliver the intended quality, safety, and efficacy profile. The stability of therapeutic proteins during storage, handling and use is a topic of intense and rich research efforts by drug product developers ( 2 ). Excipients are vital in formulating the dosage form by enhancing the manufacturability, stability, and delivery of the drug product. As complex molecules, proteins present unique considerations as active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) due to their large molecular weight, amino acid sequence, higher-order structure, post-translational modifications, co-purifying impurities, binding affinity, and biological activity. Protein formulation development can be especially challenging because, in addition to chemical degradation, protein drugs are susceptible to physical degradation of reversible and irreversible aggregates, often resulting in a significantly altered quality profile and immunological or antigenic reaction in patients ( 3 ). Thus, stable protein formulations require that both physical and chemical degradation pathways of the drug product are controlled with the diligent use of excipients ( 4 ).
16|
17|Although increased attention is now paid to excipients used in biotechnology-derived drug products, existing resources and literature on excipients rarely distinguish the use of excipients among different drug types, such as small molecule synthetic drugs and biotechnology-derived protein drugs. Such gaps in literature can drive futile efforts towards stabilizing a protein drug product when data from small molecule drug formulation studies are applied.
18|
19|We hypothesized that a comprehensive and up-to-date survey of excipients in biological therapeutic products could aid the biopharmaceutical industry by minimizing the time spent on preformulation studies to accelerate the formulation development of promising new therapeutic proteins. The ability to determine commonly used excipients or similarities across protein drug classes can aid in improving the efficiency of the formulation development process by providing evidence-driven data on the excipients associated with a known versus unknown quality profile. As well, a comprehensive survey of all excipients used in licensed/approved therapeutic biological products could theoretically provide an opportunity to extrapolate information regarding excipients that may be responsible for similar adverse reactions, interactions, or problematic quality profiles associated with various biotechnology-derived drug products. We also provide this information in the context of other known formulation parameters that impact the stability of the drug product such as concentration, pH, dosage form, and route of administration.
20|
21|In this report, we present a comprehensive survey of excipients utilized in all currently approved or licensed biotechnology-derived drug products to analyze and identify commonalities and trends among excipients used in the context of the therapeutic protein products they support. The survey includes data on active ingredients, excipients, and dosage form attributes of each drug product, to potentially enable extrapolation of information on multiple pharmaceutically relevant aspects. Our goal is to provide clarity on formulations used in marketed therapeutic proteins and improve evidence-based risk assessment for protein formulations.
22|
23|Materials and Methods
24|
25|Data Sources
26|
27|To begin curating a database of excipients used in biotechnology-derived drug products, a list of drug products currently approved or licensed by the Office of Biotechnology Products in the Office of Pharmaceutical Quality, Center for Drug Evaluation and Research (CDER) was generated. CDER’s list of licensed or approved biological products was matched with the Purple Book or the Preliminary List of Approved New Drug Applications (NDAs) for biological products ( 5 , 6 ). The current presentation and composition for each drug product was obtained from current prescribing information, publicly available on the Drugs@FDA website ( 7 ). If prescribing information (PI) was unavailable from the website, prescribing information provided by drug manufacturer websites was utilized as a secondary resource. The excipients were analyzed using a relational database program, Microsoft Access (Microsoft Corporation, Office 365 ProPlus, Access version 1902), which allowed analysis of large data through a platform and a user interface for performing targeted queries.
28|
29|Data Curation
30|
31|Since drug products manufactured in multiple drug formulations may differ in quantity or concentration of active ingredients, dosage form, package size or type of excipient, we captured unique drug formulation pertaining to the same API as a unique entry in our collection. To accurately account for these differences, data were organized by drug formulations using the assigned National Drug Code (NDC) rather than by proprietary name or active ingredient name of the drug product ( 8 ). Formulations that had the same composition and concentration of active and inactive ingredients with a different package size or type were counted as a single entry for the analysis to minimize duplication of data. For each unique formulation that was identified, all excipient names and respective quantities were recorded along with the volume of diluent to determine the concentration of each component. For the purposes of these analyses, APIs were categorized into eight different types including monoclonal antibodies, antibody conjugates, enzymes, cytokines and growth factors, polypeptide hormones, pulmonary surfactants (phospholipoproteins that reduce alveolar surface tension), recombinant fusion proteins, and toxins. Antibody conjugate molecule types included antibody-drug conjugates, antibody linker-chelator conjugates and antibody-toxin conjugates.
32|
33|Drug Product Attributes and their Categorization
34|
35|With a few exceptions, biotechnology-derived drug products are mainly delivered through the parenteral route and formulated as stable liquids or powder for reconstitution. In some cases, further dilution is required for patient-specific dosing or administration through an alternate route. Physical storage conditions such as the defined stable storage period or temperature can be different based on the presentation status of a drug formulation, whether it has been reconstituted or diluted. If a product requires reconstitution or dilution prior to the indicated route of administration, we recorded the appropriate diluent and volume that yielded the maximum allowable concentration of the active ingredient, per the label instructions. Appropriate route of administration and other physicochemical stability attributes including the pH range, requirements to protect from light, and indication not to freeze or shake the product were also recorded for the analyses.
36|
37|Consistency of Chemical Terminologies
38|
39|In order to perform consistent correlations with the data, various analogous terms used in drug labels for the same component of the drug product were evaluated and consolidated into a common term in the database prior to performing queries. The FDA’s Substance Registration System (SRS) provides unique identifiers for substances used in drugs, biologics, foods and devices based on molecular structure and descriptive information and was used as a reference ( 9 ). After consolidation of different excipient terms using SRS, a variation of hydrated forms and salt forms of ionic compounds were further consolidated into general chemical terms used in PubChem ( 10 ). Each excipient registered in the system has a Unique Ingredient Identifier (UNII) and the SRS list of synonyms was used for mapping the same excipient.
40|
41|Categorization of Excipients and Drug Products for Analyses
42|
43|Based on the protein API and context of use, a given excipient could have distinct roles in different formulations or have multiple roles in a single formulation. Therefore, when the package insert lacked clear information on the intended use, excipient monographs from the Handbook of Pharmaceutical Excipients were used to categorize each excipient based on its primary proposed physicochemical function in the appropriate dosage form ( 11 ). If an excipient monograph was unavailable for a listed excipient in the Handbook, the Encyclopedia of Pharmaceutical Technology was utilized as a secondary resource ( 12 ). We acknowledge that excipients have multiple roles, even within the same formulation, and our analyses should not be construed as an attempt to restrict the interpretation or application of these excipients. For the purposes of this analysis, excipients were categorized into 14 different functional categories (Table III ). In some instances, excipients with an undetermined role based on available resources were categorized as ‘other’ and are designated as such in the findings. Prevalence of excipients among drug formulations was determined by these functional categories and the context in which they were presented for each drug product surveyed. Graphs were generated using the Prism 6 for Windows v6.07(GraphPad Software, LaJolla, CA).
44|
45|Results
46|
47|Creating a Dataset of Excipients Used in Biotechnology-Derived Drug Products
48|
49|There are 211 biotechnology-derived APIs currently approved by CDER and marketed in the U.S. in 665 different formulations. Of 665 original formulations, 397 formulations were considered unique based on having unique excipients. The active ingredients in these formulations were categorized by molecular class, includi
