1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=33955104&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Protein Sci. 2021 Jul;30(7):1393-1407. doi: 10.1002/pro.4101. Epub 2021 May
5|19.
6|
7|Liquid-liquid phase separation of Tau by self and complex coacervation.
8|
9|Najafi S(1)(2), Lin Y(3), Longhini AP(4), Zhang X(5), Delaney KT(2), Kosik 
10|KS(4)(5), Fredrickson GH(2)(6), Shea JE(1)(7), Han S(1)(5)(6).
11|
12|Author information:
13|(1)Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of California Santa 
14|Barbara, Santa Barbara, California, USA.
15|(2)Materials Research Laboratory, University of California Santa Barbara, Santa 
16|Barbara, California, USA.
17|(3)Department of Biomolecular Science and Engineering, University of California 
18|Santa Barbara, Santa Barbara, California, USA.
19|(4)Molecular, Cell and Developmental Biology, University of California Santa 
20|Barbara, Santa Barbara, California, USA.
21|(5)Neuroscience Research Institute, University of California Santa Barbara, 
22|Santa Barbara, California, USA.
23|(6)Department of Chemical Engineering, University of California Santa Barbara, 
24|Santa Barbara, California, USA.
25|(7)Department of Physics, University of California at Santa Barbara, Santa 
26|Barbara, California, USA.
27|
28|The liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) of Tau has been postulated to play a 
29|role in modulating the aggregation property of Tau, a process known to be 
30|critically associated with the pathology of a broad range of neurodegenerative 
31|diseases including Alzheimer's Disease. Tau can undergo LLPS by homotypic 
32|interaction through self-coacervation (SC) or by heterotypic association through 
33|complex-coacervation (CC) between Tau and binding partners such as RNA. What is 
34|unclear is in what way the formation mechanisms for self and complex 
35|coacervation of Tau are similar or different, and the addition of a binding 
36|partner to Tau alters the properties of LLPS and Tau. A combination of in vitro 
37|experimental and computational study reveals that the primary driving force for 
38|both Tau CC and SC is electrostatic interactions between Tau-RNA or Tau-Tau 
39|macromolecules. The liquid condensates formed by the complex coacervation of Tau 
40|and RNA have distinctly higher micro-viscosity and greater thermal stability 
41|than that formed by the SC of Tau. Our study shows that subtle changes in 
42|solution conditions, including molecular crowding and the presence of binding 
43|partners, can lead to the formation of different types of Tau condensates with 
44|distinct micro-viscosity that can coexist as persistent and immiscible entities 
45|in solution. We speculate that the formation, rheological properties and 
46|stability of Tau droplets can be readily tuned by cellular factors, and that 
47|liquid condensation of Tau can alter the conformational equilibrium of Tau.
48|
49|© 2021 The Protein Society.
50|
51|DOI: 10.1002/pro.4101
52|PMCID: PMC8197434
53|PMID: 33955104 [Indexed for MEDLINE]

$ read_file reference/web_5390779f_eutils_efetch_fcgi_db_pubmed_id_32934220_rettype_abstract_retmode_text.txt
[exit code: 0]

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=32934220&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Nat Commun. 2020 Sep 15;11(1):4628. doi: 10.1038/s41467-020-18224-y.
5|
6|Tunable multiphase dynamics of arginine and lysine liquid condensates.
7|
8|Fisher RS(1), Elbaum-Garfinkle S(2)(3).
9|
10|Author information:
11|(1)Structural Biology Initiative, CUNY Advanced Science Research Center, New 
12|York, NY, USA.
13|(2)Structural Biology Initiative, CUNY Advanced Science Research Center, New 
14|York, NY, USA. selbaumgarfinkle@gc.cuny.edu.
15|(3)Ph.D. Programs in Biochemistry and Biology at the Graduate Center, City 
16|University of New York, New York, NY, USA. selbaumgarfinkle@gc.cuny.edu.
17|
18|Liquid phase separation into two or more coexisting phases has emerged as a new 
19|paradigm for understanding subcellular organization, prebiotic life, and the 
20|origins of disease. The design principles underlying biomolecular phase 
21|separation have the potential to drive the development of novel liquid-based 
22|organelles and therapeutics, however, an understanding of how individual 
23|molecules contribute to emergent material properties, and approaches to directly 
24|manipulate phase dynamics are lacking. Here, using microrheology, we demonstrate 
25|that droplets of poly-arginine coassembled with mono/polynucleotides have 
26|approximately 100 fold greater viscosity than comparable lysine droplets, both 
27|of which can be finer tuned by polymer length. We find that these amino 
28|acid-level differences can drive the formation of coexisting immiscible phases 
29|with tunable formation kinetics and can be further exploited to trigger the 
30|controlled release of droplet components. Together, this work provides a novel 
31|mechanism for leveraging sequence-level components in order to regulate droplet 
32|dynamics and multiphase coexistence.
33|
34|DOI: 10.1038/s41467-020-18224-y
35|PMCID: PMC7492283
36|PMID: 32934220 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
37|
38|Conflict of interest statement: The authors declare no competing interests.

$ read_file reference/web_d0a40a83_eutils_efetch_fcgi_db_pubmed_id_31958956_rettype_abstract_retmode_text.txt
[exit code: 0]

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=31958956&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. J Am Chem Soc. 2020 Feb 12;142(6):2905-2914. doi: 10.1021/jacs.9b11468. Epub 
5|2020 Jan 30.
6|
7|Multiphase Complex Coacervate Droplets.
8|
9|Lu T(1), Spruijt E(1).
10|
11|Author information:
12|(1)Institute for Molecules and Materials , Radboud University , Heyendaalseweg 
13|135 , 6525 AJ , Nijmegen , The Netherlands.
14|
15|Liquid-liquid phase separation plays an important role in cellular organization. 
16|Many subcellular condensed bodies are hierarchically organized into multiple 
17|coexisting domains or layers. However, our molecular understanding of the 
18|assembly and internal organization of these multicomponent droplets is still 
19|incomplete, and rules for the coexistence of condensed phases are lacking. Here, 
20|we show that the formation of hierarchically organized multiphase droplets with 
21|up to three coexisting layers is a generic phenomenon in mixtures of complex 
22|coacervates, which serve as models of charge-driven liquid-liquid phase 
23|separated systems. We present simple theoretical guidelines to explain both the 
24|hierarchical arrangement and the demixing transition in multiphase droplets 
25|using the interfacial tensions and critical salt concentration as inputs. 
26|Multiple coacervates can coexist if they differ sufficiently in macromolecular 
27|density, and we show that the associated differences in critical salt 
28|concentration can be used to predict multiphase droplet formation. We also show 
29|that the coexisting coacervates present distinct chemical environments that can 
30|concentrate guest molecules to different extents. Our findings suggest that 
31|condensate immiscibility may be a very general feature in biological systems, 
32|which could be exploited to design self-organized synthetic compartments to 
33|control biomolecular processes.
34|
35|DOI: 10.1021/jacs.9b11468
36|PMCID: PMC7020193
37|PMID: 31958956 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
38|
39|Conflict of interest statement: The authors declare no competing financial 
40|interest.

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=33231755&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Bull Math Biol. 2020 Nov 24;82(12):153. doi: 10.1007/s11538-020-00823-x.
5|
6|Modeling the Mechanisms by Which Coexisting Biomolecular RNA-Protein Condensates 
7|Form.
8|
9|Gasior K(1)(2), Forest MG(2)(3)(4), Gladfelter AS(5), Newby JM(6).
10|
11|Author information:
12|(1)Department of Mathematics, Florida State University, 1017 Academic Way, 
13|Tallahassee, FL, 32304, USA.
14|(2)Department of Mathematics, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, 329 
15|Phillips Hall CB #3250, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514, USA.
16|(3)Joint Department of Biomedical Engineering, University of North Carolina at 
17|Chapel Hill and North Carolina State University, 333 S Columbia Street, Chapel 
18|Hill, NC, 27514, USA.
19|(4)Department of Applied Physical Sciences, University of North Carolina at 
20|Chapel Hill, 1112 Murray Hall, CB #3050, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514, USA.
21|(5)Department of Biology, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, Coker 
22|Hall CB #3280, 120 South Road, Chapel Hill, NC, 27514, USA. amyglad@unc.edu.
23|(6)Department of Mathematical and Statistical Sciences, CAB 632, University of 
24|Alberta, Edmonton, AB, T6G 2G1, Canada. jnewby@ualberta.ca.
25|
26|Liquid-liquid phase separation is an emerging mechanism for intracellular 
27|organization. This work presents a mathematical model to examine molecular 
28|mechanisms that yield phase-separated droplets composed of different RNA-protein 
29|complexes. Using a Cahn-Hilliard diffuse interface model with a Flory-Huggins 
30|free energy scheme, we explore how multiple (here two, for simplicity) 
31|protein-RNA complexes (species) can establish a heterogeneous droplet field 
32|where droplets with single or multiple species phase separate and evolve during 
33|coarsening. We show that the complex-complex de-mixing energy tunes whether the 
34|complexes co-exist or form distinct droplets, while the transient binding 
35|kinetics dictate both the timescale of droplet formation and whether distinct 
36|species phase separate into droplets simultaneously or sequentially. For 
37|specific energetics and kinetics, a field of droplets driven by the formation of 
38|only one protein-RNA complex will emerge. Slowly, the other droplet species will 
39|accumulate inside the preformed droplets of the other species, allowing them to 
40|occupy the same droplet space. Alternatively, unfavorable species mixing creates 
41|a parasitic relationship: the slow-to-form protein-RNA complex will accumulate 
42|at the surface of a competing droplet species, siphoning off the free protein as 
43|it is released. Once this competing protein-RNA complex has sufficiently 
44|accumulated on the droplet surface, it can form a new droplet that is capable of 
45|sharing an interface with the first complex droplet but is not capable of 
46|mixing. These results give insights into a wide range of phase-separation 
47|scenarios and heterogeneous droplets that coexist but do not mix within the 
48|nucleus and the cytoplasm of cells.
49|
50|DOI: 10.1007/s11538-020-00823-x
51|PMID: 33231755 [Indexed for MEDLINE]

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=35853451&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Mol Cell. 2022 Sep 1;82(17):3193-3208.e8. doi: 10.1016/j.molcel.2022.06.024. 
5|Epub 2022 Jul 18.
6|
7|Sequence grammar underlying the unfolding and phase separation of globular 
8|proteins.
9|
10|Ruff KM(1), Choi YH(2), Cox D(2), Ormsby AR(2), Myung Y(3), Ascher DB(3), 
11|Radford SE(4), Pappu RV(5), Hatters DM(6).
12|
13|Author information:
14|(1)Department of Biomedical Engineering, Center for Science & Engineering of 
15|Living Systems, Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO 63130, USA.
16|(2)Department of Biochemistry and Pharmacology and Bio21 Molecular Science and 
17|Biotechnology Institute, The University of Melbourne, Melbourne, VIC 3010, 
18|Australia.
19|(3)Computational Biology and Clinical Informatics, Baker Heart and Diabetes 
20|Institute, Melbourne, VIC 3004, Australia; Structural Biology and 
21|Bioinformatics, Department of Biochemistry and Pharmacology, The University of 
22|Melbourne, Melbourne, VIC 3010, Australia; Systems and Computational Biology, 
23|Bio21 Institute, The University of Melbourne, Melbourne, VIC 3010, Australia.
24|(4)Astbury Centre for Structural and Molecular Biology, School of Molecular and 
25|Cellular Biology, University of Leeds, Leeds LS2 9JT, UK.
26|(5)Department of Biomedical Engineering, Center for Science & Engineering of 
27|Living Systems, Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO 63130, USA. 
28|Electronic address: pappu@wustl.edu.
29|(6)Department of Biochemistry and Pharmacology and Bio21 Molecular Science and 
30|Biotechnology Institute, The University of Melbourne, Melbourne, VIC 3010, 
31|Australia. Electronic address: dhatters@unimelb.edu.au.
32|
33|Aberrant phase separation of globular proteins is associated with many diseases. 
34|Here, we use a model protein system to understand how the unfolded states of 
35|globular proteins drive phase separation and the formation of unfolded protein 
36|deposits (UPODs). We find that for UPODs to form, the concentrations of unfolded 
37|molecules must be above a threshold value. Additionally, unfolded molecules must 
38|possess appropriate sequence grammars to drive phase separation. While UPODs 
39|recruit molecular chaperones, their compositional profiles are also influenced 
40|by synergistic physicochemical interactions governed by the sequence grammars of 
41|unfolded proteins and cellular proteins. Overall, the driving forces for phase 
42|separation and the compositional profiles of UPODs are governed by the sequence 
43|grammars of unfolded proteins. Our studies highlight the need for uncovering the 
44|sequence grammars of unfolded proteins that drive UPOD formation and cause 
45|gain-of-function interactions whereby proteins are aberrantly recruited into 
46|UPODs.
47|
48|Copyright © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
49|
50|DOI: 10.1016/j.molcel.2022.06.024
51|PMCID: PMC10846692
52|PMID: 35853451 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
53|
54|Conflict of interest statement: Declaration of interests R.V.P. is a member of 
55|the Scientific Advisory Board of Dewpoint Therapeutics Inc.

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=36513655&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Nat Commun. 2022 Dec 13;13(1):7722. doi: 10.1038/s41467-022-35370-7.
5|
6|Condensates formed by prion-like low-complexity domains have small-world network 
7|structures and interfaces defined by expanded conformations.
8|
9|Farag M(1), Cohen SR(1), Borcherds WM(2), Bremer A(2), Mittag T(2), Pappu RV(3).
10|
11|Author information:
12|(1)Department of Biomedical Engineering and Center for Biomolecular Condensates, 
13|Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO, USA.
14|(2)Department of Structural Biology, St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, 
15|Memphis, TN, USA.
16|(3)Department of Biomedical Engineering and Center for Biomolecular Condensates, 
17|Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO, USA. pappu@wustl.edu.
18|
19|Biomolecular condensates form via coupled associative and segregative phase 
20|transitions of multivalent associative macromolecules. Phase separation coupled 
21|to percolation is one example of such transitions. Here, we characterize 
22|molecular and mesoscale structural descriptions of condensates formed by 
23|intrinsically disordered prion-like low complexity domains (PLCDs). These 
24|systems conform to sticker-and-spacers architectures. Stickers are cohesive 
25|motifs that drive associative interactions through reversible crosslinking and 
26|spacers affect the cooperativity of crosslinking and overall macromolecular 
27|solubility. Our computations reproduce experimentally measured sequence-specific 
28|phase behaviors of PLCDs. Within simulated condensates, networks of reversible 
29|inter-sticker crosslinks organize PLCDs into small-world topologies. The overall 
30|dimensions of PLCDs vary with spatial location, being most expanded at and 
31|preferring to be oriented perpendicular to the interface. Our results 
32|demonstrate that even simple condensates with one type of macromolecule feature 
33|inhomogeneous spatial organizations of molecules and interfacial features that 
34|likely prime them for biochemical activity.
35|
36|© 2022. The Author(s).
37|
38|DOI: 10.1038/s41467-022-35370-7
39|PMCID: PMC9748015
40|PMID: 36513655 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
41|
42|Conflict of interest statement: R.V.P. is a member of the Scientific Advisory 
43|Board of Dewpoint Therapeutics. The work reported here was not influenced by 
44|this affiliation. The remaining authors declare no competing interests.

4|1. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 2022 Jul 12;119(28):e2202222119. doi: 
5|10.1073/pnas.2202222119. Epub 2022 Jul 5.
6|
7|Phase-separating RNA-binding proteins form heterogeneous distributions of 
8|clusters in subsaturated solutions.
9|
10|Kar M(1), Dar F(2), Welsh TJ(3), Vogel LT(4), Kühnemuth R(4), Majumdar A(1),

25|Kingdom.
26|(7)Department of Biomedical Engineering, Center for Science & Engineering of 
27|Living Systems, Washington University in St. Louis, St. Louis, MO 63130.
28|
29|Macromolecular phase separation is thought to be one of the processes that 
30|drives the formation of membraneless biomolecular condensates in cells. The 
31|dynamics of phase separation are thought to follow the tenets of classical 
32|nucleation theory, and, therefore, subsaturated solutions should be devoid of 
33|clusters with more than a few molecules. We tested this prediction using 
34|in vitro biophysical studies to characterize subsaturated solutions of 
35|phase-separating RNA-binding proteins with intrinsically disordered prion-like 
36|domains and RNA-binding domains. Surprisingly, and in direct contradiction to 
37|expectations from classical nucleation theory, we find that subsaturated 
38|solutions are characterized by the presence of heterogeneous distributions of 
39|clusters. The distributions of cluster sizes, which are dominated by small 
40|species, shift continuously toward larger sizes as protein concentrations 
41|increase and approach the saturation concentration. As a result, many of the 
42|clusters encompass tens to hundreds of molecules, while less than 1% of the 
43|solutions are mesoscale species that are several hundred nanometers in diameter. 
44|We find that cluster formation in subsaturated solutions and phase separation in 
45|supersaturated solutions are strongly coupled via sequence-encoded interactions. 
46|We also find that cluster formation and phase separation can be decoupled using 
47|solutes as well as specific sets of mutations. Our findings, which are 
48|concordant with predictions for associative polymers, implicate an interplay 
49|between networks of sequence-specific and solubility-determining interactions 
50|that, respectively, govern cluster formation in subsaturated solutions and the 
51|saturation concentrations above which phase separation occurs.
52|
53|DOI: 10.1073/pnas.2202222119
54|PMCID: PMC9282234
55|PMID: 35787038 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
56|
57|Conflict of interest statement: Competing interest statement: A.A.H. is a 
58|founder of and consultant for Dewpoint Therapeutics Inc. and a founder of 
59|Caraway Therapeutics. S.A. and R.V.P. are members of the scientific advisory 
60|board of Dewpoint Therapeutics Inc. These companies are not currently involved 
61|in the work in this paper. C.A.M.S. was involved in a collaborative review 
62|article published alongside A.D.

1|# Fetched page: https://eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/entrez/eutils/efetch.fcgi?db=pubmed&id=34931046&rettype=abstract&retmode=text
2|# Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
3|
4|1. Nat Chem. 2022 Feb;14(2):196-207. doi: 10.1038/s41557-021-00840-w. Epub 2021
5|Dec  20.
6|
7|Deciphering how naturally occurring sequence features impact the phase 
8|behaviours of disordered prion-like domains.
9|
10|Bremer A(#)(1), Farag M(#)(2), Borcherds WM(#)(1), Peran I(1), Martin EW(1), 
11|Pappu RV(3), Mittag T(4).
12|
13|Author information:
14|(1)Department of Structural Biology, St Jude Children's Research Hospital, 
15|Memphis, TN, USA.
16|(2)Department of Biomedical Engineering and Center for Science & Engineering of 
17|Living Systems (CSELS), Washington University in St Louis, St Louis, MO, USA.
18|(3)Department of Biomedical Engineering and Center for Science & Engineering of 
19|Living Systems (CSELS), Washington University in St Louis, St Louis, MO, USA. 
20|pappu@wustl.edu.
21|(4)Department of Structural Biology, St Jude Children's Research Hospital, 
22|Memphis, TN, USA. tanja.mittag@stjude.org.
23|(#)Contributed equally
24|
25|Prion-like low-complexity domains (PLCDs) have distinctive sequence grammars 
26|that determine their driving forces for phase separation. Here we uncover the 
27|physicochemical underpinnings of how evolutionarily conserved compositional 
28|biases influence the phase behaviour of PLCDs. We interpret our results in the 
29|context of the stickers-and-spacers model for the phase separation of 
30|associative polymers. We find that tyrosine is a stronger sticker than 
31|phenylalanine, whereas arginine is a context-dependent auxiliary sticker. In 
32|contrast, lysine weakens sticker-sticker interactions. Increasing the net charge 
33|per residue destabilizes phase separation while also weakening the strong 
34|coupling between single-chain contraction in dilute phases and multichain 
35|interactions that give rise to phase separation. Finally, glycine and serine 
36|residues act as non-equivalent spacers, and thus make the glycine versus serine 
37|contents an important determinant of the driving forces for phase separation. 
38|The totality of our results leads to a set of rules that enable comparative 
39|estimates of composition-specific driving forces for PLCD phase separation.
40|
41|© 2021. The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature Limited.
42|
43|DOI: 10.1038/s41557-021-00840-w
44|PMCID: PMC8818026
45|PMID: 34931046 [Indexed for MEDLINE]
46|
47|Conflict of interest statement: Competing Interests Statement R.V.P is a member 
48|of the scientific advisory board of Dewpoint Therapeutics Inc and T.M. is a 
49|consultant of Faze Medicines, Inc. The work reported here has not been 
50|influenced by either of these affiliations. All other authors declare no 
51|competing interests.
